original_abstract:
This study explores the integration of generative artificial intelligence (GAI) within higher education, specifically focusing on the faculty experience at the University of Guayaquil. The research aimed to identify the primary digital tools adopted by educators and to evaluate both the pedagogical advantages and the systemic obstacles encountered during their implementation. Utilizing a mixed-methodological approach, the study combined an extensive literature review with survey data from 334 faculty members across various disciplines. The survey instrument was designed to capture demographic profiles, existing knowledge levels, actual usage patterns, and subjective perceptions regarding the inclusion of GAI in the classroom.

The findings indicate that ChatGPT is the predominant tool in use, although adoption rates fluctuate significantly between different faculties. While GAI offers clear benefits—such as the creation of personalized learning materials and more efficient educational planning—the study identified critical barriers, most notably a pervasive resistance to change and a widespread lack of formal technical training. The analysis concludes that while generative AI holds transformative potential for enhancing university-level instruction, its success depends on strategic institutional planning, continuous professional development for faculty, and the establishment of robust ethical and pedagogical guidelines to govern its use.
humanized_abstract:
The aim of this study was to explore the integrations of generative artificial intelligence (GAI) in higher education, specifically the faculty experience at the University of Guayaquil. The main objective was to determine the main digital tools used by teachers, as well as their pedagogical benefits and barriers at the institutional level. This study uses a mixed method approach with a literature review and a survey of 334 faculty members from different academic disciplines in an attempt to get insight into their profiles, their level of technology literacy, their use of GAI based digital tools in their classrooms, and their perceptions about the adoption of GAI in higher education.

Based on the survey, ChatGPT appears to be the AI tool currently being used the most, albeit with considerable variation between the faculties. While GAI offers a number of potential advantages, such as being able to produce learning materials that can be tailored to the specific needs of individual students, as well as streamlining the administration of courses, the study also identifies a number of challenges that need to be addressed, particularly the resistance to change in some educational practices and the lack of computer literacy among certain members of the academic staff. Overall, while the study suggests that GAI has great potential for improving teaching at the university level, its implementation on a large scale will require careful planning and management, staff training, and the development of ethical and pedagogical frameworks for the effective and responsible use of these new technologies.
